title: ArrayList
subtitle: A list that grows and shrinks whenever you want — no fixed size.
goal: Create an ArrayList, add and read items, change and remove them, loop over the list, and use generics to keep it type-safe.

blocks.heading: Why arrays aren't enough 📋
blocks.html:
Remember arrays? They are great, but they have one big rule: once you make an array, its size never changes. If you make room for 3 names and a 4th friend shows up, you are stuck.

An ArrayList fixes that. It is a list that can grow when you add things and shrink when you remove them. Think of it like a to-do list on paper — you can always write one more line or cross one out.

blocks.heading: Making your first ArrayList
blocks.html:
ArrayList lives in a toolbox called java.util, so we tell Java to grab it with import at the top of the file. We import two things:

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

Then we make one like this:

List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();

The <String> part is a promise: this list will only hold Strings. That promise is called generics, and it keeps your list tidy and safe.

blocks.caption: Add names, then read the list
blocks.output:
First name: Maya
How many names? 3
All names: [Maya, Leo, Sam]
blocks.explain: add() puts something at the end of the list. get(0) reads the item at position 0 (lists start counting at 0, just like arrays). size() tells you how many items are in the list right now. Printing the whole list shows everything inside square brackets.

blocks.html: Counting starts at 0! The first item is get(0), the second is get(1), and so on. The last item is always at size() - 1.

blocks.heading: Change, remove, check, and loop
blocks.html:
An ArrayList gives you handy commands:

  • set(i, value) — replace the item at position i
  • remove(value) — take an item out (the list shrinks)
  • contains(value) — ask "is this in the list?" (gives back true or false)
  • size() — how many items are there

To visit every item, use a for-each loop: for (String task : todo) means "for each task in the todo list."

blocks.caption: A to-do list you can edit
blocks.output:
Things left to do:
- Do homework
- Feed cat
Is 'Feed cat' on the list? true
blocks.explain: set(1, ...) fixed the typo at position 1. remove("Brush teeth") took that task out, so the list shrank from 3 items to 2. The for-each loop printed each remaining task, and contains() confirmed "Feed cat" is still there.

blocks.html: Lists shrink too! After remove(), everything after the removed item slides up to fill the gap, and size() goes down by one. You never have empty holes.

blocks.heading: Lists of numbers: List<Integer> 🔢
blocks.html:
A list can hold numbers too — you write List<Integer>. Notice it is Integer with a capital I, not the little int. Generics need the full object name.

The cool part: you can still write scores.add(90) with a plain number, and Java quietly turns it into an Integer for you. That automatic wrapping is called autoboxing — you don't have to think about it.

blocks.caption: Add up a list of scores
blocks.explain: List<Integer> holds whole numbers. We added 90, 75, and 100 — autoboxing turned each plain number into an Integer automatically. The for-each loop added every score into total, then we divided by size() to get the average (265 / 3 = 88, since int division drops the leftover).

blocks.question: What is the main advantage of an ArrayList over a plain array?
options: It can change size — you can add and remove items anytime
It runs faster than everything else
It does not need to be imported
It can only hold three items
blocks.explain: Arrays have a fixed size forever. An ArrayList grows when you add and shrinks when you remove, so you are never stuck with a size that is too small or too big.

blocks.question: In List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();, what does the <String> part promise?
options: The list can hold any type at all
The list will only hold Strings (this is generics keeping it type-safe)
The list can hold exactly one item
blocks.explain: The <String> is generics. It promises the list only holds Strings, so Java catches mistakes early and you always know what is inside.

blocks.question: Which type do you use for a list of whole numbers?
blocks.explain: Generics need the object name Integer (capital I), not the small int. Thanks to autoboxing you can still call add(90) with a plain number.

blocks.title: Build a fruit list
blocks.instructions: Add "apple" and "banana" to the list using add(), in that order. Then it gets printed. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your list grew as you added each fruit!
blocks.failMsg: Use fruits.add("apple"); then fruits.add("banana"); on separate lines.

blocks.title: Loop over numbers
blocks.instructions: The list already holds 3, 6, and 9. Finish the for-each loop so it prints each number on its own line. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Great — the for-each loop visited every number in order!
blocks.failMsg: Inside the loop, write System.out.println(n); to print each number.

challenge.title: Fix and tidy a shopping list
challenge.instructions: The list has "milk", "eggs", and "bred" (a typo). Use set(2, "bread") to fix the typo, then remove("eggs") to take eggs out. Then print how many items are left and loop over each one with "- " in front. Press Check.
challenge.hint: set(2, "bread") replaces the item at position 2. remove("eggs") shrinks the list to 2 items. Then use for (String item : shopping) and print "- " + item inside.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You fixed a typo, removed an item, and looped the whole list — that is real list-wrangling. Day 17 complete!
challenge.failMsg: Use shopping.set(2, "bread"); to fix the typo, then shopping.remove("eggs"); before the loop.
